retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Prostate cancer is a disease of men. An estimated 1.2 million cases were diagnosed in 2018, making it the second most commonly diagnosed cancer in men worldwide. It was also the most common cancer in men aged over 55. In the same year, prostate cancer was the third most common cause of cancer-related death in men in North America and Europe. From a global perspective, deaths from liver, stomach, and esophageal cancer meant that it ranked sixth for cancer deaths internationally. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Prostate cancer is currently the second most commonly diagnosed cancer in men worldwide. It is an age-related disease, and the incidence is expected to rise alongside global life expectancy. The highest risk group for prostate cancer globally is African Americans living in the United States. The lowest risk group is Asian men living in their native country. [1] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Both incidence and mortality of prostate cancer vary vastly across the globe, and, as well as individual risk factors, much of this variation has been attributed to differences in asymptomatic disease screening patterns. For example, in a low-income area such as Western Africa, the age-standardized incidence rate is relatively low at 31.9 per 100,000, but the associated mortality rate is high at 18.6 per 100,000. In contrast, in a high-income area such as North America, the age-standardized incidence is much higher at 73.7 per 100,000, but the associated mortality rate is relatively low at 7.7 per 100,000. High detection rates for localized disease typically occur in high-income countries, likely due to PSA testing detecting earlier diseases with the associated reduction in mortality from treating the lower-risk disease and access to higher diagnostic and treatment capabilities. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: There have also been large variations in prostate cancer incidence worldwide over the past 30 years, further supporting the role of PSA screening in driving incidence data. Between 1986 and 1992, prostate cancer incidence doubled in the United States. This coincides with the initiation of widespread PSA screening. This spike was also seen in Europe and Australia. Incidence has started to fall, partially due to the loss of screening popularity due to over-diagnosis. In parallel, prostate cancer mortality, which had been falling over the previous two decades, has recently risen marginally. The recent US Preventative Services Task Force (PSTF) guidelines have clarified the position on PSA screening for men. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Prostate cancer incidence is generally higher in developed countries and is least common in Asian men living in Asia. However, when Asians migrate to the United States, their risk increases, although it remains lower than the general population of American men. [86] In Europe, prostate cancer is the third most diagnosed cancer after breast and colorectal cancers. [87] In the United Kingdom, it is the second most common cause of male cancer death after lung cancer, similar to the situation in the United States. [87] The WHO reports that Sweden, where they do very few PSA tests and tend to be less aggressive in treating prostate cancer, has a mortality rate that is about 2.5 times the rate in the United States. In Sweden, prostate cancer is the leading cause of cancer mortality in men, even surpassing lung cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Epidemiology -- Ethnicity
retrieved_docs.content: Mortality statistics for prostate cancer vary by ethnicity, with African Americans having the highest incidence and mortality rates, significantly surpassing the levels of the general population.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer Tissue-Based Biomarkers -- Etiology and Epidemiology -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Most men with prostate cancer will not die from the disease. In the United States, the 5-year overall survival rate is 99%. [9] The American Cancer Society has estimated that it will still kill 34,500 men out of 268,500 newly reported cases in the US in 2022. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Despite being common, prostate cancer has the highest 5-year survival of all cancers at 98%. As previously discussed, localized prostate cancer is frequently indolent and has an excellent prognosis even if left untreated. By definition, patients with high-grade, high-volume cancer are more likely to progress to locally advanced and metastatic disease, but risks can be mitigated by early detection and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most significant risk factors for prostate cancer are not modifiable; these include:

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer Tissue-Based Biomarkers -- Etiology and Epidemiology -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In 60% of countries worldwide (112/185), prostate cancer is the most commonly diagnosed malignancy. [12] Statistically, 7.3% of the total number of new cancer cases and 3.8% of the total number of deaths in 2020 were attributed to prostate cancer, as per GLOBOCAN 2020 data. [13] Worldwide incidence in 2020 was reported as 1,414,000 new cancer cases and 375,304 deaths. [14] The worldwide incidence of prostate cancer is 30.7 per 100,000, and the global mortality rate is 7.7 per 100,000. According to the World Cancer Research Fund International, the nations with the highest per capita incidence and mortality rates of prostate cancer are: Countries with the Highest Prostate Cancer Incidence per 100,000: Guadaloupe (184) Martinique (168) Ireland (111) Barbados (110) St. Lucia (103) Estonia (102) Puerto Rico (101) France (100) Sweden (99) Bahamas (98) Countries with the Highest Prostate Cancer Mortality Rate per 100,000:

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Etiology -- Race
retrieved_docs.content: Worldwide, African-American men are the most likely racial group to develop prostate cancer and are also more likely to develop the disease at a younger age, with high rates evident in Afro-Caribbean men and those of West African origin. Hispanics are at lower risk than Caucasians, as are those from South-East Asia. [1] The reasons for these differences are unclear but appear to reflect genetic predisposition and environmental factors, including diet.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The World Health Organization (WHO) reports that the countries with the highest incidence of prostate cancer are Guadeloupe, Martinique, Ireland, Barbados, Saint Lucia, Estonia, Puerto Rico, France, Sweden, and the Bahamas. Guadeloupe has the highest incidence at 184 per 100,000, whereas in the Bahamas, it drops to 98 per 100,000 compared to the global average rate of 30.7 per 100,000. The United States ranks 14th in incidence. The lowest incidence is reported in Asian countries.

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The prostate is a small glandular organ (typically 20-30 g in the healthy adult male) responsible for producing most seminal fluid. It lies anterior to the rectum and encases the urethra between the bladder neck and the external urethral sphincter. The "nervi erigentes" (responsible for continence and erectile function) course from the hypogastric plexus and lie in intimate relation to its postero-lateral surfaces. These relationships are critical when considering the likely adverse events following any treatment for prostate cancer. The zonal anatomy of the prostate was described by McNeal in 1968 and remains in use today, with around 70% to 80% of cancers developing in the peripheral zone and cancer seldom found in the central zone. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Prostate cancer is the most commonly diagnosed organ cancer in men and the second leading cause of cancer-related death among men in the United States, with lung cancer being first. [77] [78] [79]

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Etiology -- Age
retrieved_docs.content: Prostate cancer is rare under the age of 50 (1 in 350); the incidence steeply increases to 1 in 52 by age 59, and at age 65, the incidence is greater than 1 in 2, although many men will not be aware they have the disease. In the modern era of widespread serum prostate-specific antigen (PSA) testing, the majority of men have localized disease at diagnosis with a mean age of 66. [1] [9] [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer Tissue-Based Biomarkers -- Etiology and Epidemiology -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Prostate cancer is more common in developed countries than in the Third World. [11] The National Cancer Institute (NCI) has reported that the lifetime risk of acquiring clinically significant prostate cancer (Gleason 4 disease) in the US is 11.6%. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2802
retrieved_docs.content: The prostate (prostata) is the specific anatomic site associated with metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي), indicating that this malignant disease originates in the prostate gland and has the potential to spread beyond its primary location, a relationship that is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1808
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions arise within the urinary system and frequently co‑occur with prostatic disorders, seminal calculi, and both localized and metastatic prostate cancer; they are also clinically associated with prostate cancer. The prostate is an organ that belongs to the urinary system and serves as the anatomic site for prostate‑related diseases, including metastatic prostate cancer. Prostatic disorders are situated in the prostate, and other male genital disorders are likewise located in the prostate, representing subcategories of prostatic disorders. These other male genital disorders are linked to screening activities. Together, these relationships emphasize that disturbances of the prostate and associated genitourinary symptoms are central to the presentation, co‑occurrence, and clinical management of prostate disease, particularly cancer, and underscore the importance of appropriate diagnostic screening.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1087
retrieved_docs.content: The prostate (prostata) is a key anatomical site within the male genital system and is the focus of specific diagnostic procedures that are applied when the prostate tissue has been partially excised. This organ serves as the anatomic location for metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي), linking the disease directly to the prostate tissue. In addition, the prostate is identified as the location of a broader category of prostatic disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي), which functions as a parent classification for other male genital disorders. These other male genital disorders are themselves situated in the prostate and are closely associated with screening activities (التحصّي). Together, these relationships highlight the central role of the prostate in both disease manifestation and diagnostic evaluation, emphasizing the importance of targeted procedures and screening in the management of prostate‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1106
retrieved_docs.content: The prostate (prostata) serves as the anatomic site for metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي). A prostatic disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي) is located within the prostate, and other male genital disorders are likewise situated in the prostate. These other male genital disorders are classified as a subcategory of the prostatic disorder (they “have parent” اضطراب بروستاتي) and are associated with screening or preventive measures (التحصّي). Recognizing that the prostate is the primary location for both metastatic prostate cancer and various male genital disorders, and that these disorders are linked to screening activities, underscores the importance of targeted prostate evaluation and appropriate preventive strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: النَّقيلي). The clinical association of these symptom clusters with metastatic prostate cancer highlights the prostate as an anatomic site linked to advanced disease. The prostate is the anatomic locus for prostate disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي) and its subcategories, including BPH, enlargement, and cancer. Transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) is a surgical procedure performed on the prostate and is clinically associated with all of these conditions, serving both therapeutic (BPH, enlargement, cancer) and diagnostic roles. Additional male genital disorders are considered subcategories of prostate disorder, underscoring the prostate’s central role in male genitourinary health. Systematic screening (التحصّي) is linked to early detection of prostate‑related disorders. Urinary‑system interventions also encompass indwelling catheters, which are a type of urinary‑tract procedure (عملية في السبيل البولي) used to manage various urinary conditions. Collectively, the integrated anatomy of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2459
retrieved_docs.content: Prostata is identified as the anatomic site associated with سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي, indicating that prostate adenocarcinoma originates in the prostate. In addition, پروستاتا is noted as the location of اضطراب بروستاتي, reflecting that various prostatic disorders occur in this organ. Recognizing the prostate as the common site for both malignant and non‑malignant conditions highlights its clinical significance for diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 764
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions frequently co‑occur with اضطراب بروستاتي (prostatic disorder), حصاة حالبية (ureteral stone), سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي (metastatic prostate cancer) and فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد (benign prostatic hyperplasia), and they are clinically associated with سرطان البروستاتا (prostate cancer). The prostate (prostata) is the anatomic site linked to سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي and it houses اضطراب بروستاتي. The prostate also serves as the location for other male genital disorders and for the surgical procedure transurethral resection of prostate (TURP). TURP is clinically associated with فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد, تضخم بروستاتي (prostatic enlargement) and سرطان البروستاتا, reflecting its role in treating both benign hyperplasia and prostate cancer. Other male genital disorders are categorized under اضطراب بروستاتي, are located in the prostate, and are associated with التحصّي (screening). Understanding these overlapping clinical presentations, anatomical relationships, and therapeutic links supports more comprehensive patient assessment and management in urologic practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 641
retrieved_docs.content: Prostatolithotomy, prostatotomy with calculus removal, and transurethral prostate incision are all classified as “other or therapeutic procedures; male genital,” and each of these interventions is performed directly on the prostate. The prostate itself is identified as the anatomic site associated with metastatic prostate cancer and serves as the location for prostatic disorders. Other male genital disorders are likewise situated in the prostate, are considered a subset of prostatic disorders, and are linked to screening activities. Together, these relationships highlight the prostate as a central focus for both disease processes—such as metastatic cancer and various prostatic disorders—and for therapeutic procedures aimed at treating conditions within this organ.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1926
retrieved_docs.content: Prostata is described as having the location identified as اضطراب بروستاتي, indicating that prostatic disorders are situated within the prostate. Other male genital disorders are located in the prostata, are classified as a subcategory under اضطراب بروستاتي (having اضطراب بروستاتي as their parent), and are associated with التحصّي, suggesting a link to preventive screening or immunization measures. Recognizing that other male genital disorders arise in the prostate, fall under the broader category of prostatic disorder, and are connected with التحصّي informs clinical assessment, diagnosis, and preventive care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2529
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures; male genital refer to examinations and interventions performed on male genital organs, and the prostate (prostata) is a partially excised anatomy that is commonly involved in such diagnostic procedures. The prostate (prostata) is also the anatomic site associated with metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي). Recognizing that the prostate can be partially removed for diagnostic evaluation and that it serves as the primary location for this malignant disease is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 152
retrieved_docs.content: biopsy (خزعة الرحم) and endometrial biopsy. General prostatic disorders (اضطراب بروستاتي) are placed under “other male genital disorders,” associated with screening/preventive measures (التحصّي) and connected to the prostate through a location‑of relationship. Collectively, these relationships provide a structured framework that maps biopsy and other diagnostic methods to specific organ systems, classifies therapeutic procedures within gender‑specific categories, and integrates oncologic entities such as malignant prostate tumors and their patterns of spread, thereby supporting comprehensive clinical decision‑making and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1886
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions are noted to co‑occur with حصاة حالبية and also with سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي, indicating that patients who present with vague or nonspecific genitourinary complaints may simultaneously have ureteral stones (حصاة حالبية) or prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي). Recognizing this association can prompt clinicians to consider targeted diagnostic evaluations for these specific urologic pathologies when faced with ambiguous genitourinary presentations, thereby improving detection and management of underlying stone disease or malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2262
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions frequently co‑occur with حصاة حالبية (ureteral stone), سرطـان البروستاتا النَّقيلي (metastatic prostate cancer), and فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد (benign prostatic hyperplasia). This pattern of co‑occurrence indicates that patients who present with nonspecific genitourinary complaints may also have underlying stone disease, advanced prostate malignancy, or benign prostatic enlargement, underscoring the need for thorough diagnostic assessment to identify and manage these potentially related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 227

This integrated framework illustrates how bladder disorders, prostate disorders, urinary stones, and prostate malignancies interrelate, and how a spectrum of diagnostic and therapeutic procedures—particularly those directed at the prostate—are organized to manage both benign and malignant conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 446
retrieved_docs.content: erectile dysfunction (خلل وظيفي في الانتصاب), underscoring the need for careful patient counseling and postoperative management. Overall, the interrelationships among the urogenital system, prostate cancer, its surgical and diagnostic interventions, and associated anatomical sites provide a comprehensive framework for understanding disease presentation, treatment strategies, and potential complications in male urogenital health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 162
retrieved_docs.content: the primary site of prostatic disorder and related male genital diseases. Common pathologies include benign prostatic hyperplasia (فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد), prostatic enlargement (تضخم بروستاتي), and prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا, سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي). Therapeutic strategies comprise transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) for BPH and related enlargement, open prostatectomy (which may involve partial removal of adjacent structures such as the seminal vesicles), and transurethral prostate incision. Urinary tract procedures (عملية في السبيل البولي) frequently employ an indwelling catheter as a parent entity, and systematic screening (التحصّي) of male genital disorders emphasizes early detection and monitoring of prostate and related conditions, illustrating the integrated anatomy, pathology, and treatment framework in male genitourinary care.
